mainEntity.name: What's the best macro lens for a beginner?

acceptedAnswer.text: A 90mm or 100mm macro lens is the best all-round starting point. The working distance of around 15cm at 1:1 means you can photograph insects without scaring them, and the focal length works for flowers, textures, and general close-ups. Sigma 105mm, Tamron 90mm, Nikon 105mm, and Canon 100mm are all excellent options at various price points.

mainEntity.name: Can I do macro photography with my phone?

acceptedAnswer.text: Modern iPhones (13 Pro and later) and Pixel phones have usable macro modes for casual shots, but they hit a ceiling fast. Phone macro is typically 1:3 or 1:4 at best, and the small sensor struggles with the shallow depth of field macro demands. A second-hand camera body with a used 60mm macro lens will cost around £500 and transform your results.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a tripod for macro photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: For static subjects — flowers, water droplets, indoor setups — yes, a tripod transforms results. For garden insects and moving subjects, handheld is the only realistic option because insects don't wait for you to set up. Buy a decent tripod when you start, but don't let the lack of one stop you from shooting handheld.

mainEntity.name: What aperture should I use for macro?

acceptedAnswer.text: f/8 to f/11 is the sweet spot for most macro work — enough depth of field to get the subject's main features sharp, while avoiding the diffraction that softens images at f/16 and smaller. For artistic, selective-focus shots, f/2.8 to f/5.6 works. For very small subjects that need edge-to-edge sharpness, explore focus stacking.

mainEntity.name: How do I keep insects in the frame long enough to photograph?

acceptedAnswer.text: Shoot in the early morning when insects are cool, slow, and far easier to approach. Move slowly — no sudden motions, no shadows falling across the subject, no breathing on it. Use a longer macro lens (90mm or 105mm) so you can work from a distance. Accept that you'll lose plenty of subjects and come back tomorrow.

mainEntity.name: What ISO can I use before images get noisy?

acceptedAnswer.text: Modern cameras produce clean images up to ISO 1600, and often to ISO 3200 with some noise reduction in post. Don't be afraid to raise ISO for a faster shutter speed — a sharp, slightly noisy image is always better than a soft, clean one. Noise reduction in Lightroom or free tools like Darktable handles ISO 1600 noise comfortably.

mainEntity.name: Why are my macro photographs always slightly soft?

acceptedAnswer.text: The usual culprit is shutter speed. At 1:1 magnification, camera shake is magnified enormously — 1/125s feels fast but often isn't enough. Try 1/500s handheld as a minimum. If your shots are still soft on a tripod, check that mirror-lock-up or electronic shutter is enabled to eliminate mirror slap vibration.

mainEntity.name: What's focus stacking and do I need to learn it?

acceptedAnswer.text: Focus stacking is taking multiple frames at different focus distances and combining them in software for edge-to-edge sharpness. It's extremely useful for static subjects like insects on cold mornings or still-life work, but it's a rabbit hole. Get your first hundred handheld macro keepers before you invest time in stacking.
